payload.instructions:
# Repository Guidelines

## Project Structure & Module Organization
- `src/core/`: ECC math, jump tables, DP detection (`Ec.cpp`, `unified_jump_table.cpp`)
- `src/gpu/`: CUDA host/device glue + kernels (`GpuKang.cpp`, `RCGpuCore.cu`, `RCGpuUtils.h`)
- `src/precompute/`: table generation/query/compression (`table_generator.cpp`, `Table*Kernel.cu`)
- `src/main/`: CLI entrypoint (`src/main/RCKangaroo.cpp`)
- `tests/`: small C++ integration tests (no full harness)
- `external/`: reference code (treat as read-only)

## Build, Test, and Development Commands
- `make`: build `rckangaroo`, `table_generator`, `table_repacker`
- `make clean`: remove `*.o` and test binaries
- `make test-integration`: build + run `tests/test_integration_precompute.cpp`
- Example solve: `./rckangaroo -range 84 -dp 16 -start 0x... -pubkey 03...`
- Example table: `./table_generator -range 48 -dp 16 -T 65536 -compress seed -seed 1 -output table.ptam`

## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
- Tabs for indentation, same-line braces, C++17 throughout
- Use `u32/u64` etc from `src/core/defs.h`; avoid STL in CUDA kernels
- Types: PascalCase; globals: `gPrefix`; constants: `UPPER_SNAKE`
- Code comments in English; docs/PR descriptions in 简体中文; use neutral identifiers (“puzzle/target”)

## Testing Guidelines
- Prefer small ranges first (`-range 40..60`) before long runs
- Use `cuda-memcheck ./rckangaroo ...` for GPU memory issues; compare throughput/K against prior logs

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
- Keep commits small and performance-scoped (e.g., `Precompute: fix seed table repack`)
- Don’t include generated artifacts in diffs (`*.o`, `rckangaroo`, `RESULTS.TXT`, `table_*.bin`, tame tables)
- If touching hot paths, include a short benchmark note (GPU model + keys/s or DPs/s)


## Skills
These skills are discovered at startup from multiple local sources. Each entry includes a name, description, and file path so you can open the source for full instructions.
- skill-creator: Guide for creating effective skills. This skill should be used when users want to create a new skill (or update an existing skill) that extends Codex's capabilities with specialized knowledge, workflows, or tool integrations. (file: /mnt/d/mybitcoin/2/cuda-bsgs-production-full/RCKangaroo/.codex/skills/.system/skill-creator/SKILL.md)
- skill-installer: Install Codex skills into $CODEX_HOME/skills from a curated list or a GitHub repo path. Use when a user asks to list installable skills, install a curated skill, or install a skill from another repo (including private repos). (file: /mnt/d/mybitcoin/2/cuda-bsgs-production-full/RCKangaroo/.codex/skills/.system/skill-installer/SKILL.md)
- Discovery: Available skills are listed in project docs and may also appear in a runtime "## Skills" section (name + description + file path). These are the sources of truth; skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.
- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.
- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.
- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):
  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.
  2) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.
  3) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.
  4) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.
- Description as trigger: The YAML `description` in `SKILL.md` is the primary trigger signal; rely on it to decide applicability. If unsure, ask a brief clarification before proceeding.
- Coordination and sequencing:
  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.
  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.
- Context hygiene:
  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.
  - Avoid deeply nested references; prefer one-hop files explicitly linked from `SKILL.md`.
  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.
- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.
